taskContext.task: Wave 1 Task 2 - Define scenario taxonomy and target capability layer model
taskContext.purpose: Provide a canonical, classification-ready model centered on planning, audit, and debug default flows.
inputMissing: .sisyphus/drafts/ecosystem-audit-cohesion.md (not found in repository at execution time)

modelIntent.scope: Skill/package classification model for runtime capability layering
explicitlyInScope: Relationship model between core flows and secondary specialist flows
Classification guidance that can be applied in Task 3
explicitlyOutOfScope: Creating a universal ontology outside this audit objective

designPrinciples: Default runtime must optimize for planning, audit, and debug loop completion.
Secondary scenarios should attach to core flows, not replace them.
Auto-activation surface should be intentionally narrow to reduce false activation.
Preserve non-core capabilities via manual or dormant tiers before prune decisions.
Reject breadth-biased assumption that every on-disk skill needs implied dynamic activation evidence.

coreDefaultScenarios.name: Planning
coreDefaultScenarios.objective: Turn ambiguous requests into scoped, executable tasks with explicit constraints and verification targets.
coreDefaultScenarios.classificationSignal: Capability directly increases plan quality, decomposition quality, or execution readiness in routine tasks.

coreDefaultScenarios.name: Audit
coreDefaultScenarios.objective: Inspect repo/config/runtime evidence to validate alignment, detect drift, and identify highest-signal remediation priorities.
requiredOutcomes: Actionable next steps with minimal safe changes
coreDefaultScenarios.classificationSignal: Capability primarily improves evidence gathering, comparison, drift detection, prioritization, or governance confidence.

coreDefaultScenarios.name: Debug
coreDefaultScenarios.objective: Diagnose failures systematically, isolate root causes, apply minimal fixes, and verify closure.
coreDefaultScenarios.classificationSignal: Capability materially improves triage, diagnosis, fix strategy, or verification reliability under failure conditions.

secondaryScenarios.name: Browser QA
secondaryScenarios.objective: Validate behavior in real browser flows, especially for UI regressions and interaction bugs.
secondaryScenarios.promotionRule: Can influence default only for assets that are universally needed to complete core debug/audit loops; otherwise remains manual specialist support.

secondaryScenarios.name: Architecture Deep-Dive
secondaryScenarios.objective: Evaluate multi-module/system trade-offs beyond routine planning scope.
secondaryScenarios.promotionRule: Stays manual unless evidence shows repeated necessity in everyday planning for this ecosystem.

secondaryScenarios.name: Incident Response
secondaryScenarios.objective: Coordinate urgent diagnosis/recovery under service instability or outage-like pressure.
secondaryScenarios.promotionRule: Manual by default due to lower frequency; promote only if incident mode becomes routine in default operations.

secondaryScenarios.name: Specialist Framework/Domain Work
secondaryScenarios.objective: Handle language/framework/business-domain specific tasks not central to default ecosystem operation.
secondaryScenarios.promotionRule: Do not place in default unless required for the majority of default planning/audit/debug flows.

coreSecondaryRelationshipRules: Core scenarios define the default runtime spine: planning -> audit -> debug.
Secondary scenarios are overlays activated by explicit intent or concrete contextual evidence.
A secondary capability cannot be default only because it exists or has broad triggers.
Secondary scenario assets should degrade to manual before dormant, and to candidate-prune only after redundancy/low-value evidence accumulates.

handoffExpectations: Planning must provide audit/debug-ready constraints and acceptance criteria.
Audit findings feed planning refinements or direct debug priorities.
Debug resolution feeds back into audit confidence and planning patterns.

secondaryInjectionPoints.browser-qa-secondary: Inject after audit or during debug verification when browser behavior is material.
secondaryInjectionPoints.architecture-deep-dive-secondary: Inject during planning when scope spans multiple subsystems or ADR-level trade-offs.
secondaryInjectionPoints.incident-response-secondary: Inject when failure urgency/coordination needs exceed routine debug flow.
secondaryInjectionPoints.specialist-framework-secondary: Inject only when explicit framework/domain requirements are present.

default.definition: Auto-available baseline capability required to reliably execute the core planning/audit/debug loop in normal operation.
default.defaultActivationRule: Enabled by default and eligible for first-pass routing without explicit user opt-in.
entryCriteria: Primary scenario alignment to at least one core scenario (planning-core, audit-core, debug-core)
Recurring necessity across routine tasks, not niche episodes
Low false-activation risk relative to other enabled defaults
Provides distinct value (not redundant overlap with existing default capabilities)
exitCriteria: No longer needed for routine core loop completion
Shows high overlap or ambiguity that harms routing precision
Better positioned as explicit specialist/manual invocation
default.demotionCondition: When core-loop necessity weakens or ambiguity cost rises, demote to manual.
default.reactivationCondition: Promote from manual when repeated core-loop dependency is demonstrated with low activation noise.

manual.definition: Available capability for secondary or specialized scenarios that should require explicit intent/context before use.
manual.defaultActivationRule: Not pre-activated by default; activated through explicit user request or high-confidence specialist context.
entryCriteria: Secondary scenario alignment (browser QA, architecture deep-dive, incident response, specialist framework/domain)
Useful but not essential for routine default core loop
Preserves value without polluting default surface
exitCriteria: Demonstrated routine core-loop necessity (promote to default)
Sustained low-demand or weak scenario fit (demote to dormant)
manual.demotionCondition: If rarely invoked and not needed for core flows, demote to dormant.
manual.reactivationCondition: Promote to default only with evidence of repeated default-flow necessity; otherwise remain manual.

dormant.definition: Retained but inactive capability kept for contingency, migration safety, or low-frequency edge need.
dormant.defaultActivationRule: Disabled from normal routing and default loading; available only via explicit reactivation path.
entryCriteria: Not required for default or regular manual specialist operation
Still has plausible fallback, compatibility, or occasional recovery value
exitCriteria: Revalidated scenario value and usage confidence (promote to manual)
No meaningful value across review cycles (demote to candidate-prune)
dormant.demotionCondition: If dormant utility cannot be justified during periodic review, move to candidate-prune.
dormant.reactivationCondition: Promote to manual when a concrete scenario requires it and value is validated in use.

candidate-prune.definition: Low-value or redundant capability with no compelling scenario role; staged for removal unless new evidence appears.
candidate-prune.defaultActivationRule: Never default-activated; excluded from runtime routing except explicit review contexts.
entryCriteria: No strong alignment to core or secondary scenarios
Negligible demand or persistent activation noise cost
No strategic reason to keep as dormant fallback
exitCriteria: Evidence-backed scenario requirement reappears (promote to dormant/manual)
No reactivation case after review window (proceed to removal workflow)
candidate-prune.demotionCondition: If no valid reactivation case is found in review, remove from active ecosystem surfaces.
candidate-prune.reactivationCondition: Only with explicit, current scenario evidence and non-redundant value; re-enter via dormant or manual first.

policyNotes: Demote in stages to preserve optionality and reduce abrupt capability loss.
Require stronger evidence for promotion as tier impact increases.
Avoid direct candidate-prune -> default jumps unless emergency and explicitly justified.

decisionQuestions: Which scenario does this asset primarily serve: planning-core, audit-core, debug-core, or a secondary scenario?
Is this asset necessary for routine core-loop completion, or only specialized contexts?
If always enabled, does it increase false activation/routing ambiguity?
Is the asset meaningfully distinct, or mostly redundant with current capabilities?
If demoted, is fallback retention needed (dormant) or is prune candidacy more appropriate?
tierAssignmentHeuristics: Core-aligned + routine necessity + low noise => default
Low-frequency fallback without active demand => dormant
No scenario fit or clear redundancy => candidate-prune
antiPatternsToAvoid: Classifying by legacy category label alone
Promoting assets to default because they are broadly worded
Treating low usage as immediate deletion without dormant staging
Keeping candidate-prune assets indefinitely without revalidation
